active_paradigms.description: Replace SLSQP with differential_evolution which is a stochastic global optimizer that doesn't rely on gradients. This can escape local optima that trap SLSQP. Encode the problem as: variables are 52 position coordinates + 26 radii. Objective: minimize negative sum of radii. Constraints: use penalty formulation or the bounds/constraints mechanism in differential_evolution. The 'polish' option can refine with L-BFGS-B at the end. Use strategy='best1bin' with appropriate mutation and recombination parameters. This is fundamentally different from gradient-based SLSQP.
active_paradigms.what_to_optimize: sum_radii - global optimization to escape local optima
active_paradigms.cautions: differential_evolution is slower than SLSQP, so reduce maxiter appropriately. Use the 'constraints' parameter with NonlinearConstraint for circle non-overlap. The search space is 78-dimensional which is large, so use tight bounds and good population size.

active_paradigms.description: Replace SLSQP with the trust-constr method which handles nonlinear constraints more robustly. trust-constr uses a trust-region approach with conjugate gradient for subproblems, often performing better on problems with many nonlinear constraints. Keep the same initialization and LP refinement structure. Use NonlinearConstraint objects for the circle overlap constraints and box constraints. The trust-constr algorithm is particularly good when constraints are active at the solution, which is the case for dense packings.
active_paradigms.what_to_optimize: sum_radii - better handling of active constraints at optimum
active_paradigms.cautions: trust-constr requires constraints in a specific format using NonlinearConstraint or LinearConstraint. May need more iterations than SLSQP. The Jacobian and Hessian of constraints can be provided for better performance, but numerical approximations work too.

active_paradigms.idea: Use greedy sequential placement with scipy.optimize.minimize for each circle
active_paradigms.description: Instead of optimizing all 26 circles simultaneously, place circles one at a time greedily. For each new circle, solve a small optimization problem: maximize its radius subject to not overlapping with already-placed circles and staying in the square. Use a strategic ordering: largest circles first in corners/edges, then fill interior. After all circles placed, do a final joint refinement. This is fundamentally different from simultaneous optimization and can find solutions that joint optimization misses.
active_paradigms.cautions: Order matters significantly - try different orderings (corners first, largest potential radius first, etc.). The greedy solution may not be globally optimal, so a final joint optimization pass is essential. Use scipy.optimize.minimize with SLSQP for each single-circle placement problem.

tried_paradigms.description: Design explicit contact graph: specify which circle pairs should touch (e.g., corner circles touch 2 edges + 2 neighbors, interior circles touch 6 neighbors in hexagonal pattern). For each touching pair, write equation r_i + r_j = dist(i,j). For boundary-touching circles, add r_i = min(x_i, y_i, 1-x_i, 1-y_i). This creates linear system Ar = b where A has rows like [1, 1, 0, ...] for pair constraints. Solve using scipy.linalg.lstsq for least-squares solution. If solution violates non-touching pair constraints (r_i + r_j > dist(i,j)), adjust topology by removing problematic contacts. Iterate until valid solution found. This converts nonlinear optimization to linear algebra via fixed topology.
tried_paradigms.what_to_optimize: sum of radii through optimal contact topology design
tried_paradigms.cautions: Topology must be geometrically realizable - not all contact graphs can be embedded in unit square. Start with sparse topology (fewer contacts) and add contacts incrementally. The linear system may be under/over-determined - lstsq handles both. Verify final solution satisfies ALL constraints including non-touching pairs. May need topology search over multiple candidate graphs. Corner circles naturally have higher degree in contact graph.

tried_paradigms.description: Replace trust-constr with SLSQP (Sequential Quadratic Programming). SLSQP builds and solves quadratic subproblems at each iteration, which can find different local optima than trust-constr's barrier method. Reformulate constraints as dictionaries: {'type': 'ineq', 'fun': constraints_fun} for SLSQP compatibility. Keep the same objective (negated sum of radii) and LP refinement step for final radii. SLSQP often converges faster and may escape different local minima.
tried_paradigms.what_to_optimize: primary sum_radii metric through different optimization path
tried_paradigms.cautions: SLSQP requires constraints in dictionary format, not NonlinearConstraint objects. Ensure Jacobian is provided for efficiency. May need different tolerance settings (ftol, maxiter).

tried_paradigms.description: Use COBYLA (Constrained Optimization BY Linear Approximation) which maintains a simplex of points and approximates both objective and constraints linearly. Since COBYLA doesn't support bounds directly, add bound constraints to the constraint function: for each circle i, add constraints ri - xi <= 0, ri - yi <= 0, ri + xi - 1 <= 0, ri + yi - 1 <= 0. COBYLA's linear approximation approach can be more robust for highly nonlinear problems and may find solutions trust-constr misses.
tried_paradigms.what_to_optimize: primary sum_radii through fundamentally different optimization algorithm
tried_paradigms.cautions: COBYLA doesn't accept bounds parameter or Jacobian. Must encode all bounds as inequality constraints. Use rhobeg parameter to control initial simplex size. May need more iterations.

tried_paradigms.idea: Corner-anchored geometric construction with LP refinement
tried_paradigms.description: Directly construct positions exploiting square geometry: (1) Place 4 corner circles at positions (r_corner, r_corner), (1-r_corner, r_corner), etc. with radius ~0.146 each (touching two walls). (2) Place ~12 edge circles along the 4 edges, each touching one wall. (3) Place remaining 10 circles in interior using hexagonal pattern. Use scipy.optimize.linprog to compute optimal radii for these fixed positions. This exploits the square's structure that the current hexagonal pattern ignores.
tried_paradigms.what_to_optimize: primary sum_radii through better initial geometry that exploits corner/edge structure
tried_paradigms.cautions: Corner circles need careful placement to not overlap with edge circles. Edge circle spacing must account for corner circles. Interior circles need to fit between edge circles. Use LP with all pairwise distance constraints.

tried_paradigms.idea: Use scipy.optimize.fmin_cobyla for derivative-free constrained optimization
tried_paradigms.description: Replace SLSQP with COBYLA (Constrained Optimization BY Linear Approximation). COBYLA is derivative-free and uses linear approximations of constraints, which can handle non-convex landscapes differently than gradient-based SLSQP. Build constraint functions for each circle pair (no overlap) and each circle boundary (inside unit square). COBYLA constructs and solves linear programming subproblems at each iteration, exploring the feasible region in a fundamentally different way. Use the same corner initialization and LP radii solver, but let COBYLA optimize positions.
tried_paradigms.what_to_optimize: sum of radii through better position optimization
tried_paradigms.cautions: COBYLA requires constraint functions that return values >= 0 for feasible points. Build constraints as: distance(i,j) - r[i] - r[j] >= 0 for no overlap, and min(x-r, y-r, 1-x-r, 1-y-r) >= 0 for boundary. Use rhobeg=0.1 for initial step size, maxfun=50000 for sufficient exploration. May need multiple random starts.

tried_paradigms.idea: Use scipy.optimize.minimize with Nelder-Mead and penalty formulation for constraint handling
tried_paradigms.description: Reformulate the constrained problem as unconstrained using quadratic penalties. The objective becomes: minimize -sum(radii) + penalty * sum(constraint_violations^2). Nelder-Mead is a simplex-based derivative-free method that explores by reflecting, expanding, and contracting a simplex in parameter space. This geometric exploration can escape local optima that trap gradient methods. Use high penalty (10000+) to enforce constraints. The key advantage is that Nelder-Mead doesn't follow gradients, so it can traverse flat regions and saddle points that would stop SLSQP.
tried_paradigms.what_to_optimize: sum of radii through global exploration of position space
tried_paradigms.cautions: Penalty parameter must be large enough to enforce constraints but not so large it causes numerical issues. Start with penalty=10000 and increase if constraints are violated. Use adaptive simplex parameters: initial_simplex with edge length 0.1, xatol=1e-10, fatol=1e-10, maxiter=50000. Multiple restarts from different initializations recommended.

tried_paradigms.idea: Direct geometric construction with optimized lattice parameters
tried_paradigms.description: Instead of optimizing all 52 position variables, construct the packing directly from a small set of geometric parameters. For n=26: place 4 corner circles at (r_c, r_c), (1-r_c, r_c), etc. Place edge circles at calculated positions based on spacing parameter s_e. Place interior circles in a perturbed hexagonal lattice with spacing s_i. Then use LP to solve for optimal radii given these positions. Optimize only the 3-5 key parameters (r_c, s_e, s_i) using a simple grid search or scipy.optimize.minimize on this reduced space. This drastically reduces dimensionality and exploits geometric structure.
tried_paradigms.what_to_optimize: key geometric parameters that determine the entire packing structure
tried_paradigms.cautions: The structure must accommodate exactly 26 circles. One viable structure: 4 corners + 8 edge (2 per edge) + 14 interior. Calculate edge positions as midpoints between corners adjusted by spacing. For interior, use hexagonal lattice with origin at (0.5, 0.5). Ensure parameter ranges produce valid packings: r_c in [0.08, 0.15], s_e in [0.1, 0.4], s_i in [0.08, 0.2]. Validate each constructed packing with LP before scoring.

tried_paradigms.idea: Use differential evolution global optimizer with penalty-based constraint handling
tried_paradigms.description: Replace SLSQP with scipy.optimize.differential_evolution for true global optimization. Define a single penalty function that encodes all constraints: penalty = sum(max(0, r_i + r_j - d_ij)) for overlaps + sum(max(0, r_i - min_dist_to_wall)) for boundary violations. Optimize positions AND radii jointly (78 variables total: 52 for positions, 26 for radii). Use polynomial mutation and recombination with population size 15-20, maxiter 1000. The global search explores the solution space more thoroughly than gradient-based SLSQP starting from fixed initializations.
tried_paradigms.what_to_optimize: Maximize sum of radii while satisfying all non-overlap and boundary constraints
tried_paradigms.cautions: Differential evolution is slower than SLSQP, so limit maxiter to fit within timeout. Use bounds [0.01,0.99] for positions and [0.01,0.2] for radii. The penalty function must be smooth enough for the optimizer to navigate. Use seed for reproducibility.

tried_paradigms.idea: Use hexagonal lattice perturbation with basin-hopping global optimization
tried_paradigms.description: Initialize circles in a perturbed hexagonal lattice arrangement (optimal for infinite packing). For n=26, use a 5-6-5-5-5 row pattern or similar hexagonal arrangement. Then apply scipy.optimize.basinhopping which combines local minimization (L-BFGS-B) with random jumps to escape local minima. The key is that basin-hopping explores the energy landscape globally, finding better packings than pure local optimization. Use 50-100 basin-hopping iterations with T=0.5 temperature parameter.
tried_paradigms.what_to_optimize: Find global optimum of circle packing through landscape exploration
tried_paradigms.cautions: Basin-hopping requires a good local minimizer. Use L-BFGS-B with bounds. The objective should include penalty terms for constraint violations. Test different temperature values (0.3-1.0) for the random jump acceptance.

tried_paradigms.idea: Use dual annealing for constraint-satisfying global optimization
tried_paradigms.description: Apply scipy.optimize.dual_annealing which combines simulated annealing with local search. Define the objective as -sum(radii) with constraint violations added as large penalties. Dual annealing is particularly good at escaping local minima in constrained problems. Use the default parameters with maxiter=2000. The method performs global exploration via simulated annealing then refines with local L-BFGS-B. This is fundamentally different from SLSQP's gradient descent approach.
tried_paradigms.what_to_optimize: Global optimization of circle positions and radii to maximize sum
tried_paradigms.cautions: Dual annealing can be slow; set appropriate maxiter. Use bounds for all variables. The penalty for constraint violations should be large enough (e.g., 100x the typical radius) to force the optimizer toward feasible regions. May need multiple runs with different seeds.
